core_sentence_architecture.core_meaning: Identifying or naming a person, thing, or concept.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: When you want to say what something or someone is.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: Subject + be + not + noun ; Question: Be + subject + noun?
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Pronouns, common nouns (teacher, student, book, dog), jobs, nationalities.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Omitting the article ("I am teacher"), confusing is/are, using "be" incorrectly.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I'm a ...", "It's a ...", "Are you a ...?"
examples: I am a student.
She is my friend.
They are doctors.

core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: To describe people, things, situations, or feelings.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: not + adjective; Question: Be + subject + adjective?; very/really + adjective.
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Common adjectives (happy, big, tired, hungry, beautiful, cold, important).
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using "is" for all subjects, adding "-ing" to adjectives unnecessarily, forgetting "be".
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I'm ...", "It's really ...", "Are you ...?"
examples: I am tired.
This book is interesting.
They are happy today.

core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Saying what someone or something does.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: For simple present actions, habits, general truths.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: don't/doesn't + base verb; Question: Do/Does + subject + base verb?
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: High-frequency verbs (eat, go, live, work, like, have, play, speak, want).
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Forgetting -s for third person singular, using "do" in affirmative statements, double negatives.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I live in ...", "She works at ...", "Do you like ...?"
examples: I play football.
He works in a hospital.
They speak English very well.

core_sentence_architecture.core_meaning: Stating where someone or something is.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: To describe position, place, or origin.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: not + there; Question: Be + subject + preposition + place?; common prepositions: in, on, at, under, next to.
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Place nouns (room, city, table, park), prepositions of place.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Wrong preposition (in/on/at), omitting "be", word order (place before be).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "It's in the ...", "I'm at ...", "Where are ...?"
examples: The cat is under the table.
We are at the park.
She is in London.

core_sentence_architecture.structure: There is / There are + noun (+ location)
core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Introducing a new thing or person into conversation.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: To say that something exists, especially in a place.
core_sentence_architecture.pattern: There is + singular/uncountable noun; There are + plural noun (+ in/on/at place)
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: There isn't / There aren't; Question: Is there / Are there?; There's a lot of...
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Common nouns (book, people, milk, problem), place names, quantifiers (a, some, any, many).
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using "have" instead of "there is/are" (e.g., "The room has a table"), agreement errors (there is + plural noun).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "There's a ... in my ...", "Are there any ...?"
examples: There is a cat in the garden.
There are three books on the desk.
Is there any milk in the fridge?

core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Talking about what you have, characteristics, or family.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: Informal spoken British English or to describe current possession, appearance, or relationships.
core_sentence_architecture.pattern: I/You/We/They + have got; He/She/It + has got + [noun/noun phrase]
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: haven't got / hasn't got; Question: Have/Has + subject + got?; contraction: 've got, 's got.
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Possessions (car, phone), family members (brother, sister), physical features (blue eyes, long hair).
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using "got" alone without have/has, using "have got" for past possession, forgetting "got" in questions.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I've got a ...", "Has she got ...?", "He's got ..."
examples: I have got a new phone.
She has got two brothers.
Have you got any money?

core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Stating what you own, family, or attributes (more universal/formal than have got).
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: All registers, esp. American English; for habits, general facts.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: don't have / doesn't have; Question: Do/Does + subject + have?; have + abstract noun (I have an idea).
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Same as have got, plus abstract nouns (time, problem, appointment).
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using have got in formal writing, incorrect auxiliary in negatives (using "haven't" instead of "don't have" for habitual possession).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I have a question", "Do you have a ...?", "She doesn't have ..."
examples: I have a dog.
He doesn't have any brothers.
Do you have time for a coffee?

core_sentence_architecture.core_meaning: A person/thing does something to someone/something.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: When the action directly affects an object.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: don't/doesn't + verb + object; Question: Do/Does + subject + verb + object?; object pronouns (me, him, it).
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Transitive high-frequency verbs (eat, drink, read, watch, need, want, love, like, help).
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Omitting the object when needed, incorrect object pronoun form (she likes he).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I like ...", "She wants ...", "Do you need ...?"
examples: I like pizza.
He reads books every day.
She doesn't watch TV.

core_sentence_architecture.structure: Subject + action verb + indirect object + direct object
core_sentence_architecture.core_meaning: Giving or communicating something to someone.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: With verbs like give, send, show, tell, buy, bring.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Alternative: Subject + verb + direct object + to/for + indirect object (give the book to Mary); pronouns order.
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Dative verbs (give, send, tell, show, buy, bring, make, offer), people, common objects.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Wrong preposition (I gave to him the book), pronoun order (I gave it him -> I gave it to him).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "Can you give me ...?", "I'll show you ...", "She sent me a ..."
examples: I gave my mother a present.
She sent me an email.
Can you tell me your name?

core_sentence_architecture.core_meaning: Action happening right now, temporary situation.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: For actions in progress at the moment of speaking, or around now.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: am not/isn't/aren't + verb-ing; Question: Are/Is + subject + verb-ing?; time markers (now, right now, at the moment).
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Action verbs (doing, going, eating, reading, waiting, working, studying).
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using present simple for now (I eat now), forgetting be (I going), stative verbs in continuous (I am wanting).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I'm working on ...", "What are you doing?", "She's coming."
examples: I am reading a book right now.
He is sleeping.
What are you doing?

core_sentence_architecture.structure: Be + subject + complement? (Yes/No question with be)
core_sentence_architecture.core_meaning: Asking about identity, state, or location.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: To get a yes/no answer about a person's or thing's nature.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Short answers: Yes, I am. / No, he isn't.; negative questions: Isn't he a teacher?
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: As in statements.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using DO (Do you be ...?), not inverting (You are tired?).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "Are you okay?", "Is it ready?", "Am I late?"
examples: Are you a doctor?
Is she hungry?
Are they at home?

core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Checking if something is true or happens.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: For yes/no questions about present actions.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Short answers: Yes, I do. / No, she doesn't.; negative questions: Don't you like it?; subject question: Who eats apples? (no do).
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: High-frequency action verbs.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using DO with be (Do you are...), forgetting base after does (Does she likes...), missing auxiliary.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "Do you speak ...?", "Does he live ...?", "Do I need ...?"
examples: Do you like coffee?
Does she work here?
Do they have a car?

core_sentence_architecture.core_meaning: Asking for specific information about identity, location, quality, etc.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: To ask who, what, where, when (with be), why, how.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Who as subject (Who is that?); Which + noun; How + adjective (How old are you?).
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Wh-words (what, where, who, when, why, how), nouns, adjectives.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Forgetting be (Where you from?), wrong word order (You are from where?).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "Where are you from?", "What's your name?", "How are you?"
examples: Where are you from?
What is your job?
How old is she?

core_sentence_architecture.structure: Wh-word + do/does + subject + base verb + ...? (Wh-question with other verbs)
core_sentence_architecture.core_meaning: Asking for information about actions or facts.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: To ask what, where, when, why, how about someone's actions.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: When the wh-word is the subject: Who eats? (no do). How often, how many, etc.
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Wh-words, action verbs, frequency expressions.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: No do-insertion (Where you live?), using verb form (Where do you lives?), double mark.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "Where do you live?", "What do you do?", "How do you spell ...?"
examples: Where do you work?
What does she want?
How do you go to school?

core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Talking about skills, what is possible, or offering help.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: For present abilities, informal permission, or suggestions.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: cannot/can't; Question: Can + subject + base verb?; can for requests (Can you open the door?).
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Skill verbs (swim, drive, speak, cook), sense verbs (see, hear).
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Adding -s in third person (She cans swim), using to after can, mixing with present continuous.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I can help you.", "Can you ...?", "I can't ..."
examples: I can swim.
She can speak three languages.
Can you open the window?

core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: To say what activities/things you enjoy or not.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Do you like?; really like; prefer; would like + to-inf (for specific desire).
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Hobby nouns and verbs (reading, music, football, cooking, dogs).
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using to-infinitive after like for general preference (I like to swim. — acceptable but verb-ing more common for general hobbies), omitting -ing.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I like watching movies.", "Do you like ...?", "She loves ..."
examples: I like swimming.
She hates getting up early.
Do you like pizza?

core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Expressing what you wish to have or do.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: For desires, intentions, making polite requests (I want you to...).
core_sentence_architecture.pattern: I/You/We/They want; He/She/It wants + [noun] or [to + base verb]
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: don't/doesn't want; Question: Do you want ...?; Want + someone + to (I want you to come).
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Nouns (drink, break), action verbs.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Want + that-clause (I want that you go), forgetting -s, using to with noun (I want to water).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I want to go home.", "Do you want some tea?", "She wants a ..."
examples: I want a glass of water.
He wants to leave now.
She doesn't want to eat.

core_sentence_architecture.core_meaning: Making a suggestion involving the speaker and listener.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: When you want to include everyone in an activity.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Let's not; Shall we? tag; How about + verb-ing? alternative.
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Activity verbs (go, eat, start, take, dance).
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Adding -s, using to after let's, using with third person (Let's he goes...).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "Let's go!", "Let's have lunch.", "Let's not be late."
examples: Let's take a taxi.
Let's not wait.
Let's eat.

core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: Commands, recipes, directions, offers, invitations.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: Don't + base verb; Polite with "please"; Let's (including speaker).
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Action verbs (stop, open, sit, turn, come).
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using subject you (You sit down), forgetting negative form (No open).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "Come in!", "Turn left.", "Don't worry."
examples: Open your books.
Don't touch that.
Please sit down.

core_sentence_architecture.structure: Subject + would like + noun / to + verb
core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Making polite requests, offers, invitations (more polite than "want").
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: In formal/customer service settings, to sound polite.
core_sentence_architecture.pattern: [Subject] + would like ('d like) + [noun] or [to + base verb]
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Would you like...? for offers; I'd like to have...
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Items (menu, drink), activities.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using like without would (I like a coffee now — sounds general preference), using -ing after would like.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I'd like a coffee, please.", "Would you like to come?", "She'd like to speak to ..."
examples: I would like some water.
Would you like a biscuit?
He'd like to see the manager.

core_sentence_architecture.core_meaning: Making a general statement about an action.
core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Evaluating an activity, giving opinion about a process.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: To say that doing something is easy, important, difficult, etc.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: It's + adjective + for someone + to; negative: not + adjective.
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Evaluative adjectives (easy, hard, important, necessary, fun, possible).
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using that-clause (It is important that...), missing to, using for incorrectly.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "It's nice to meet you.", "It's hard to say.", "Is it safe to ...?"
examples: It's easy to make mistakes.
It is important to be on time.
It's not difficult to learn.

core_sentence_architecture.structure: Subject + be + going to + verb (future plan/intention)
core_sentence_architecture.core_meaning: Future plan or intention, or prediction based on present evidence.
core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Talking about future arrangements or obvious predictions.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: When you have already decided to do something or see something about to happen.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Question: Are you going to...?; negative: not going to; gonna (spoken).
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Action verbs.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using will for everything, forgetting be, using going to go excessively (I'm going to go shopping -> I'm going shopping also possible).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I'm going to call you.", "It's going to rain.", "Are you going to ...?"
examples: I'm going to visit my grandmother.
She is going to buy a new car.
Look at those clouds! It's going to rain.

core_sentence_architecture.structure: Subject + be + past simple of be (was/were)
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: For past descriptions, jobs, feelings, places.
core_sentence_architecture.pattern: I/He/She/It + was; We/You/They + were (+ noun/adjective/prep phrase)
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: wasn't/weren't; Question: Was/Were + subject...?
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Past time expressions (yesterday, last night, in 2010), same complements.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using was with plural (we was), using be in past with did (Did you be...?).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I was tired.", "Were you at home?", "It was great."
examples: I was at school yesterday.
They were very happy.
Was it cold last night?

core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: For finished past actions at a specific time.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: didn't + base verb; Question: Did + subject + base verb?; time markers (yesterday, last week, ago).
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: High-frequency past verbs (went, did, had, bought, ate, saw, came, took).
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using base after did in questions (Did you went?), using past in negative after didn't, over-regularizing irregulars (goed).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I went to ...", "Did you see ...?", "She didn't come."
examples: I bought a new phone.
He didn't eat breakfast.
Did you watch the film?

core_sentence_architecture.structure: There was / There were + noun (+ location)
core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Talking about what was present at a past time/place.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: For describing past scenes, reporting past existence.
core_sentence_architecture.pattern: There was + singular/uncountable noun; There were + plural noun.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: wasn't/weren't; Question: Was/Were there?; Some, any, many.
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Past time expressions, place, nouns.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using there is for past, forgetting agreement with plural.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "There was a problem.", "Were there many people?", "There wasn't any milk."
examples: There was a big storm last night.
There were many people at the party.
Was there any coffee?

core_sentence_architecture.core_meaning: Experience, recent past with relevance, unfinished time.
core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Talking about life experience, recent news, result now.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: For events without a specific past time, or with "ever, never, just, already, yet".
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: haven't/hasn't; Question: Have/Has + subject + past participle?; been vs gone.
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Past participles of common verbs (eaten, seen, been, done, worked), time markers.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using with specific past time (I have seen him yesterday), wrong participle forms.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I've lost my keys.", "Have you ever been to ...?", "She hasn't arrived yet."
examples: I have visited Paris twice.
She has just finished her homework.
Have you ever eaten sushi?

core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Making promises, offers, predictions without evidence, instant decisions.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: When deciding now, promising, or predicting based on opinion.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: won't; Question: Will + subject + base verb?; 'll contraction.
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Action verbs.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using will for pre-arranged plans, forgetting base form, using will in if-clauses (If I will...).
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "I'll call you later.", "Will you help me?", "I think it will rain."
examples: I will help you with that.
I think she will pass the exam.
Will you be home tonight?

core_sentence_architecture.structure: Subject + can / could / may + base verb (permission/request)
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: Can (informal), could (polite), may (formal).
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Answers: Sure, Of course, No problem; negative: can't, may not.
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Action verbs.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using "can I to...", mixing with be able to unnecessarily, over-formal.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "Can I ask a question?", "Could you repeat that?", "May I come in?"
examples: Can I use your phone?
Could you open the door, please?
May I sit here?

core_sentence_architecture.structure: Subject + must / have to + base verb (obligation)
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: Must (internal obligation, speaker's authority); have to (external obligation, general rule).
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Negative: mustn't (prohibition), don't have to (not necessary); need to (necessity).
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Obligation verbs.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using must for past (had to), confusing mustn't and don't have to.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "You must wear a seatbelt.", "I have to go now.", "Do we have to ...?"
examples: You must stop here.
I have to finish this report.
She doesn't have to work tomorrow.

core_sentence_architecture.primary_communicative_function: Talking about causing someone to act.
core_sentence_architecture.when_to_use: Let = allow; Make = force; Help = assist.
core_sentence_architecture.key_variations: Help + to + verb also possible (more formal). Negative: don't let.
core_sentence_architecture.essential_vocabulary_slots: Verbs of action.
core_sentence_architecture.common_learner_mistakes: Using to after let/make (She let me to go), incorrect object pronoun.
core_sentence_architecture.chunk_compatibility: "Let me think.", "My mom makes me clean my room.", "Can you help me carry this?"
examples: She let me drive her car.
The teacher made us rewrite the test.
He helped me move the furniture.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Permanent truths, habits, routines.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Stating general truths, describing daily life.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: With adverbs of frequency, general facts, schedules.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: always, usually, often, sometimes, never.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.essential_vocabulary_slots: Routine verbs (wake up, get, go, have, start, finish).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Missing -s, using present continuous for habits.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I usually get up at 7.", "She never eats meat.", "Water boils at 100 degrees."
examples: The sun rises in the east.
He drinks coffee every morning.
Do you play tennis on Sundays?

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Action happening at the moment of speaking.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: With now, right now, at the moment; temporary situations.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Time markers; negative: not + verb-ing; questions.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using with stative verbs (I am wanting), omitting be.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I'm working at the moment.", "It's raining.", "What are you doing?"
examples: She is cooking dinner now.
They are not listening.
Are you waiting for someone?

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Fixed future plan (social/professional).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Talking about definite plans in near future.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: When time and place are decided (diary future).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Going to vs Present Continuous for plans.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.essential_vocabulary_slots: Verbs of movement/activity (meeting, going, seeing, having, leaving).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Forgetting time marker, using for general future without arrangement.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I'm meeting her tomorrow.", "We're flying to Rome next week.", "He's having dinner with friends tonight."
examples: She's starting a new job on Monday.
What are you doing this weekend?
I'm not working next Friday.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Finished actions at a specific past time.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: With yesterday, last night, ago, in 2010, when I was a child.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Regular -ed, irregular forms; negatives with didn't; questions with Did.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.essential_vocabulary_slots: Common past verbs (went, did, had, saw, ate, got, took).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using present after did (Did you went?), forgetting time marker for clear past.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I saw a movie last night.", "She didn't come.", "When did you arrive?"
examples: We moved here five years ago.
He forgot his keys.
They didn't enjoy the party.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Action in progress at a specific past time, background activity.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: When another action happened, longer action in past.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: While/as; interrupted by Past Simple; all morning/day.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using for short completed events, forgetting was/were.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I was sleeping when you called.", "What were you doing at 8pm?"
examples: At 9 o'clock I was working.
She was cooking while he was watching TV.
They weren't listening during the meeting.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Past habit or state no longer true.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: To talk about discontinued habits/states.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Negative: didn't use to; Question: Did you use to...?
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.essential_vocabulary_slots: Past activities, states (live, smoke, play, be).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Use to in present context, writing "use to" in affirmative.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I used to play the piano.", "She didn't use to like coffee.", "Did you use to live here?"
examples: My dad used to smoke.
This building used to be a cinema.
I didn't use to enjoy studying.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Experience without specifying when.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Asking and talking about ever/never experiences.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: With ever, never, before, superlatives (the best I have ever ...).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Have you ever ...?, She's never + past participle.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Adding a specific time (I have ever been to Paris in 2015).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "Have you ever tried sushi?", "I've never been to Asia.", "It's the best movie I've ever seen."
examples: I have never ridden a horse.
Has she ever met a celebrity?
We have visited London twice.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Recent events, result, expectation.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: Just (very recent), already (sooner than expected), yet (expected, in questions/negatives).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.pattern: [Subject] + have/has + just/already + past participle; (negative) haven't/hasn't + past participle + yet
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Yet at end of sentence.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.essential_vocabulary_slots: Verbs of completion (finish, eat, see, arrive).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Yet in positive statements, word order of just/already (before participle).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I've just finished.", "She's already left.", "Have you eaten yet?"
examples: I've just seen Paul.
We haven't decided yet.
He has already paid the bill.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Action/state continuing from past to present.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Saying how long something has been true.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: With verbs that can continue (live, work, know, have, be).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: for + period; since + point in time. Present Perfect Continuous possible with action verbs.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using present simple (I live here since 2010), mixing for and since.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I've known her for ten years.", "He's worked here since 2018.", "How long have you been here?"
examples: I have lived in this city since 2015.
She has had this car for two months.
How long have you known each other?

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Past before past.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Showing sequence of two past events.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: When one past action happened before another past action.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.pattern: [Subject] + had + [past participle] + when/by the time/before + Past Simple
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Already, just, never with Past Perfect.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using Past Perfect without second past action, overuse when order is clear.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "When I arrived, the train had already left.", "She had never seen snow before she moved to Canada."
examples: I had finished my work before the meeting started.
He realized he had lost his wallet.
By 10 pm, they had already gone to bed.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Prediction based on opinion (will) vs evidence (going to).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: Use going to when you see something now; will for general opinion.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.pattern: I think + will; Look at... + going to.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Might for uncertain predictions.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using will when there is clear evidence.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I think it will rain." vs "Look! It's going to rain."
examples: I think Brazil will win the match.
She's going to have a baby. (seen her belly)
The traffic is terrible. We're going to be late.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Timetabled future events.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: For fixed timetables (transport, movies, classes).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: The train leaves at 6 pm; What time does the show start?
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using will for timetables (The train will leave at 6) unnatural.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "The bus leaves at 8.", "My flight arrives at noon."
examples: The meeting starts at 9 am tomorrow.
What time does the film begin?
School finishes on 15th June.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Action in progress at a future time.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Describing ongoing future activity, polite enquiries about plans.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: At this time tomorrow, Will you be using...? (polite)
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: By this time next week; might be + -ing.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using for simple future events.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I'll be waiting outside.", "This time tomorrow I'll be lying on the beach."
examples: Don't call at 8; I'll be eating dinner.
Will you be passing the supermarket?
Next month we'll be living in Madrid.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.structure: be going to vs Present Continuous for plans
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Both for plans; going to more intention, PC more fixed arrangement.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Distinguishing between decided intention and definite plan.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: Going to for intention (I've decided), PC for diary arrangement (specific place/time).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.pattern: I'm going to travel next month. / I'm travelling to Berlin on Friday.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Both acceptable in many contexts.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using PC for strong intention without arrangement, using will for everything.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I'm going to start a course." vs "I'm starting the course on Monday."
examples: I'm going to learn Italian (intention). I'm starting lessons on Tuesday (arrangement).
We're going to buy a new car. We're seeing the dealer tomorrow.
She's going to invite them. She's having a party on Saturday.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: General truth, scientific fact.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Talking about things that always happen.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: Cause-effect that is always true.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: When instead of if.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using will in both clauses.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "If you heat water, it boils.", "If I sleep late, I feel tired."
examples: If you mix blue and yellow, you get green.
When it rains, the streets get wet.
If I don't eat breakfast, I get hungry.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Real/possible future condition and result.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: To discuss plans, warnings, promises.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Will can be replaced by can, might, imperative; unless.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using will in the if-clause, confusing with zero.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "If it rains, I'll take an umbrella.", "I'll call you if I'm late.", "If you don't hurry, we'll miss the bus."
examples: If you study hard, you will pass the exam.
She will be angry if you forget her birthday.
If I see him, I'll tell him.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Unreal, imaginary, or unlikely present/future situation.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: To talk about things that are not true or very unlikely.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: If I were you, I'd... (advice). Could/might instead of would.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using would in if-clause, using present for unreal.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "If I had more money, I'd travel.", "If I were you, I'd talk to her.", "What would you do if you won the lottery?"
examples: If I knew the answer, I would tell you.
She would be happier if she lived near her family.
If he were here, he'd help us.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.structure: Third conditional (If + had + past participle, would have + past participle)
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Imaginary past situation, regret.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Talking about how things could have been different.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: To express past unreal conditions.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Might have, could have.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Wrong structure, using second conditional for past.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "If I had studied harder, I would have passed.", "I wouldn't have done that."
examples: If we had left earlier, we wouldn't have missed the flight.
She would have helped if you had asked.
If I had known, I would have told you.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.structure: Mixed conditional (If + past perfect, would + base)
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Past condition with present result.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Explaining a present situation as result of past event.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: When a past action affects now.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Not always if-clause first.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using it for past result only.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "If I had saved more money, I wouldn't be broke now.", "If she had taken the job, she'd be in London."
examples: If you had listened to me, you wouldn't be in this mess.
I would be rich if I had bought that stock.
If he'd been born in Italy, he would speak Italian.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Desire for a different present reality.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: To talk about things you want to be different now.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: I wish I could...; If only...
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using present after wish (I wish I am...), using would wish.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I wish I had a bigger house.", "I wish I could swim.", "I wish it were Friday."
examples: I wish I knew the answer.
She wishes she didn't have to work.
We wish we lived near the beach.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Regret about something that happened or didn't happen.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: To express regret about past actions.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: If only...
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using present perfect or past simple.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I wish I had studied more.", "I wish you hadn't said that."
examples: I wish I had taken the job.
She wishes she had listened to her mother.
They wish they hadn't moved.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Desire for someone/something to change behavior or for something to happen.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: When you are annoyed or want a different future action.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: I wish it would stop...
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using with I (I wish I would...) usually not.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I wish you would listen.", "I wish it would stop raining.", "I wish he wouldn't talk so loudly."
examples: I wish the bus would come.
I wish my neighbors would be quieter.
Do you ever wish people would just leave you alone?

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Reporting what someone said, with tense shift.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: To tell someone what else someone said.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Told + person; present simple -> past simple, will -> would, can -> could, etc.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Not backshifting, mixing direct speech, pronoun changes missed.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "He said he was tired.", "She told me she would call.", "They said they had finished."
examples: She said (that) she liked the movie.
He told me he had already eaten.
They said they were moving to Canada.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Focus on action/object rather than doer.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.primary_communicative_function: When the agent is unknown, unimportant, or obvious.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: News, processes, formal English.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Modal passives (can be done); present perfect passive (has been done).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Overusing by-phrase, forgetting be, using intransitive verbs.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "It was made in China.", "English is spoken here.", "The window was broken."
examples: My car was stolen last night.
The report hasn't been finished yet.
This room is cleaned every day.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Arranging for someone else to do something for you.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: When you don't do it yourself.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Get something done (informal); need something done.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Thinking it's passive about subject (I had cut my hair vs I had my hair cut).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I had my hair done.", "She's having her house painted.", "Where do you get your car fixed?"
examples: I need to have my eyes tested.
We had our roof repaired last month.
He's getting his suit cleaned.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Comparing two things (more...).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: When two items are different.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: much/a lot/a bit + comparative; as...as for equality.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.essential_vocabulary_slots: Common adjectives (taller, more expensive, better, worse).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Double comparative (more taller), using as...than, forgetting than.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I'm taller than you.", "This is more interesting than that.", "It's better than before."
examples: My car is faster than yours.
This book is more difficult than the last one.
She's younger than me.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: The highest degree in a group.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Stating the number one in quality.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: To describe extremes within a set.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: One of the + superlative + plural noun.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.essential_vocabulary_slots: Extreme adjectives (best, worst, biggest, most beautiful).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Forgetting "the", using comparative instead of superlative.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "She's the smartest in the class.", "This is the best pizza ever.", "It's the most expensive hotel."
examples: Russia is the largest country.
He's the funniest person I know.
This is the worst day of my life.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Inferior or not equal comparison.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Saying something is less or not equal.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: To deny equality.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: less + adjective + than (formal); as...as affirmative (He's as tall as me).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using not so...as (acceptable but less taught), incorrect adjective form.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "It's not as good as I thought.", "She's not as old as her sister."
examples: This phone isn't as expensive as that one.
I'm not as tired as yesterday.
The exam was not as difficult as we expected.

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Equality in quality or quantity.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: When two things are equal.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: as much/many as.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using than instead of as.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "You're as tall as your dad.", "It's as good as new.", "He can't run as fast as his brother."
examples: She's as smart as her sister.
I work as hard as you.
This cake is not as sweet as the last one. (negative equality)

essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.core_meaning: Checking information or inviting agreement.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.when_to_use: After statements, expecting yes/no.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.key_variations: Tags with auxiliary (aren't you?, isn't it?, didn't you?, can't you?, shall we?).
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Wrong polarity (You're tired, isn't it?), wrong pronoun.
essential_tense_and_aspect_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "You're coming, aren't you?", "It's cold, isn't it?", "You don't know, do you?"
examples: He's a teacher, isn't he?
You won't forget, will you?
They went home, didn't they?

modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Talking about what you can do.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: General ability, with verbs of skill.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Adding to, third person -s.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I can drive.", "Can you swim?", "She can't cook."
examples: I can play the guitar.
Can he speak Spanish?
She can't see without glasses.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Among friends, children, casual settings.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: Can you... for requests; Yes, you can. / No, you can't.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using may in casual speech (sounding too formal), can to go? word order.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Can I open the window?", "Can I use your charger?"
examples: Can I go out tonight?
Can we sit here?
You can take my pen.

modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Talking about skills in the past.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: With ability in the past (not a single event, that's managed to).
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: Negative couldn't; question Could you...? for past.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using could for one specific event (I could escape -> I managed to escape).
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I could run fast when I was a kid.", "She couldn't swim until she was 10."
examples: When I was five, I could already read.
He couldn't cook before he got married.
Could you speak English as a child?

modal_and_functional_structures.core_meaning: Polite request, more formal than can.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: With strangers, in service.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using can when you need to be polite, adding please at beginning only.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Could you help me?", "Could you say that again?", "Could I speak to ...?"
examples: Could you close the door, please?
Could you tell me the time?
Could I have a glass of water?

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: In formal meetings, letters, with authority.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using may in casual speech, confusing with might.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "May I come in?", "May I have your attention, please?", "You may leave now."
examples: May I use your restroom?
May I ask who is calling?
Students may not use phones in class.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: When you're unsure (small chance).
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using may and might interchangeably with no nuance, though often okay.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I might go to the party.", "It might rain later.", "She might not come."
examples: He might be in his office.
I might be late tomorrow.
We might not have enough time.

modal_and_functional_structures.core_meaning: Decision made at the moment of speaking.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: I'll get it! I'll help you! (not planned).
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: I think I'll...; shall I? (offer, British).
modal_and_functional_structures.essential_vocabulary_slots: Immediate action verbs (get, open, answer, help).
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using going to for instant decisions.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'll answer the phone.", "I'll have the fish, please.", "I'll carry that for you."
examples: The doorbell's ringing. I'll get it.
It's cold. I'll close the window.
We're lost. I'll check the map.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: I will always love you, I'll be there.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: I promise (that) I will... / I'll + verb
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: I won't tell anyone. I'll make sure.
modal_and_functional_structures.essential_vocabulary_slots: Verbs of promise (call, help, never forget).
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using present continuous for promise.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'll call you tomorrow.", "I won't be late.", "I'll do my best."
examples: I'll send you the pictures.
Don't worry, I won't forget.
I'll always be there for you.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: If I were..., I would.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using would in if-clause.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'd travel if I had money.", "She wouldn't do that.", "What would you do?"
examples: I would live by the sea if I could.
He wouldn't understand.
Would you take the job?

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: To offer drinks, invitations.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: Would you like + noun/to verb?; Would you + verb + ...?
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using Do you want in formal.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Would you like some coffee?", "Would you like to dance?", "Would you join us?"
examples: Would you like a cup of tea?
Would you like to come to dinner?
Would you help me with this?

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: I must remember, you must see this.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using must for past (had to), mustn't vs don't have to.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I must call my mom.", "You must try this cake.", "We mustn't be late."
examples: You must be here by 8.
I must finish this report.
You mustn't tell anyone.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: School, work, laws.
modal_and_functional_structures.essential_vocabulary_slots: Verbs of duty (wear, follow, work, attend).
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using must for rules (both possible but have to more external).
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I have to wear a uniform.", "Do you have to work weekends?", "She doesn't have to come."
examples: You have to drive on the left in the UK.
He has to take medicine every day.
We don't have to stay until the end.

modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Telling what's forbidden and what's not required.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Mustn't = it's not allowed; don't have to = you can if you want but not necessary.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: Mustn't + base verb; don't/doesn't have to + base verb.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Swapping meanings, e.g., You don't have to smoke here (meaning You mustn't).
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You mustn't park here.", "You don't have to bring a gift.", "You mustn't use your phone."
examples: You mustn't run by the pool. / You don't have to swim if you don't want to.
We mustn't be noisy. / We don't have to whisper.
You mustn't tell her. / You don't have to tell her.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: I think you should, maybe, perhaps.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Adding to, using should in past without have (should went).
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You should rest.", "I think we should leave.", "Should I call him?"
examples: You should see a doctor.
He shouldn't eat so much sugar.
What should I do?

modal_and_functional_structures.core_meaning: Strong advice, threat of negative consequence.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: When something bad may happen if you don't.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using to, using for general advice when should is enough.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You'd better hurry.", "We'd better not be late.", "I'd better go."
examples: You'd better take an umbrella — it looks like rain.
I'd better not forget my passport.
He'd better apologize or she'll be upset.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: More common than must/have to in informal.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: don't need to = not necessary; needn't (formal); need + noun.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Need + -ing? No, need + to base.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I need to buy some milk.", "Do you need to take a break?", "She needs to talk to you."
examples: I need to go to the bathroom.
You don't need to bring anything.
He needs to get a job.

modal_and_functional_structures.structure: Be able to (ability in various tenses)
modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Express ability in forms where can is not possible (future, perfect).
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Will be able to, have been able to, used to be able to.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: be (am/is/are/was/were/will be) + able to + base verb
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using can for future (I will can), not knowing past single event (was able to manage to).
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I've never been able to understand.", "Will you be able to come?", "She wasn't able to finish."
examples: I hope to be able to travel soon.
He hasn't been able to sleep.
Were you able to get tickets?

modal_and_functional_structures.core_meaning: What is expected according to rules, plans.
modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Saying what should happen (often negative: but it didn't).
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Rules, arrangements, commonly known duties.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using supposed followed by infinitive without to, forgetting d (suppose to).
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You're supposed to knock.", "It was supposed to rain.", "What are we supposed to do?"
examples: The train is supposed to arrive at 5.
You're not supposed to park here.
I was supposed to call her yesterday, but I forgot.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: In rules, when you can't use can/may for tenses.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using allowed to + -ing, missing be.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Are we allowed to take photos?", "We weren't allowed to enter.", "You're not allowed to smoke."
examples: You're allowed to use a dictionary.
He wasn't allowed to go out.
Will we be allowed to film?

modal_and_functional_structures.core_meaning: Preference for one thing over another.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: I'd rather stay home; I'd rather you didn't.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: [Subject] + would rather ('d rather) + base verb (+ than) / + subject + past simple (other person preference)
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: I'd rather not; I'd rather you went.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using to base, past form wrongly when referring to other.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'd rather walk.", "I'd rather not talk about it.", "She'd rather you called later."
examples: I'd rather have tea than coffee.
He'd rather stay home tonight.
I'd rather you didn't smoke in here.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: For habitual preferences.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: Prefer something to something; prefer doing to doing.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Prefer... than (should be to), using to-infinitive after prefer.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I prefer coffee to tea.", "She prefers walking.", "Do you prefer driving or flying?"
examples: I prefer summer to winter.
He prefers watching TV to reading.
Most people prefer living in the city.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: When consequences are serious.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Not + better? No, better not.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You'd better not be late.", "I'd better not forget my keys."
examples: You'd better not tell her.
We'd better not miss the bus.
He'd better not eat that.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Must be (certain true), might be (possible), can't be (certain false).
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: He must be tired, That can't be true.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using mustn't for deduction (mustn't is prohibition), confusing with can't.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You must be tired.", "That can't be right.", "He might be lost."
examples: She must be at work now.
It can't be that hard.
They might be sleeping.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Informal group planning.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: Why don't we + verb; How about + verb-ing; What about + noun.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using let's with tag isn't it, wrong intonation.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Let's take a break, shall we?", "Why don't we go for a walk?", "How about eating out?"
examples: Let's watch a film, shall we?
Why don't we try the new restaurant?
How about a cup of tea?

modal_and_functional_structures.core_meaning: Polite offer (I) or suggestion (we), British English.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Offering to do something for someone, asking for decision.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: Shall I + base verb? / Shall we + base verb?
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using will I for offers.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Shall I open the window?", "Shall we dance?", "What shall we do tonight?"
examples: Shall I help you with that?
Shall we order pizza?
Shall I call a taxi?

modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Asking permission in a delicate way.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: When you might disturb someone.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: Do you mind if I + present; Would you mind if I + past.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Answering yes (means I do mind = no), confusing negative answer.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Would you mind opening the window?", "Do you mind if I sit here?", "Would you mind helping me?"
examples: Would you mind closing the door?
Do you mind if I use your phone?
I hope you don't mind my asking.

modal_and_functional_structures.structure: Is it okay if I...? / Can I...? variations
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: With friends, family.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: Is it okay/ alright if I + [present simple]?
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: Can I; Could I; Do you mind if.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Overusing may in casual settings.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Is it okay if I bring a friend?", "Can I borrow your pen?", "Is it all right if I leave early?"
examples: Is it okay if I open the window?
Can I have a glass of water?
Would it be okay if I called you later?

modal_and_functional_structures.structure: Might want to / might as well
modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Giving gentle advice, suggesting a logical course.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: "It's late, we might as well go." / "You might want to check that."
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: [Subject] + might want to + base verb / might as well + base verb
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using should when might want to is softer.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You might want to hurry.", "We might as well wait.", "I might as well tell you."
examples: You might want to see a doctor.
There's no bus, so we might as well walk.
I might as well finish this now.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Look at those clouds.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: She's going to have a baby.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using will instead, missing be.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "It's going to rain.", "He's going to fall!", "That chair is going to break."
examples: Look out! That cup is going to fall.
The traffic is terrible. We're going to be late.
She's going to love this present.

modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Talking about what is familiar, not strange anymore.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: To contrast past difficulty with present ease.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: get used to (process), not used to.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Confusing with "used to" for past habit, missing verb-ing.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'm used to the weather.", "She's not used to driving on the left.", "Are you used to the noise?"
examples: I'm used to waking up early.
He's not used to spicy food.
They got used to living in a small town.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: I don't think; I think so; I thought (past).
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Doubling negative (I think I don't like it. -> I don't think I like it).
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I think it's a good idea.", "I don't think she's home.", "I think so."
examples: I think you're right.
I don't think it will rain.
I think it's time to go.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Casual way to say what you want.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.when_to_use: To suggest an activity or state.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: I don't feel like; Do you feel like?
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using to verb, using with clause (I feel like I need... okay but different nuance).
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I feel like pizza.", "I don't feel like going out.", "Do you feel like a walk?"
examples: I feel like a cup of coffee.
She feels like dancing.
I don't feel like working today.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.structure: I'm good at / bad at + noun/verb-ing
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: pretty good, terrible at, not bad at.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using to verb, forgetting -ing.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I'm good at sports.", "She's bad at keeping secrets.", "Are you good at drawing?"
examples: I'm good at cooking.
He's terrible at singing.
She's not very good at multitasking.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.core_meaning: Desire for someone else to act.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.when_to_use: Telling someone what you wish they'd do.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: Would like you to; expect you to.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using that-clause (I want that you help), omitting to.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I want you to meet someone.", "She wants me to go.", "Do you want him to call?"
examples: I want you to listen carefully.
He wants me to help him.
They want us to arrive early.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Talking about how long something requires.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: It takes me 10 minutes; How long does it take?
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using for (It takes for me), wrong word order.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "It takes 20 minutes to walk there.", "It takes me an hour to get ready.", "How long does it take to fly to Paris?"
examples: It takes about 15 minutes to cook.
It took her two hours to finish.
It doesn't take long to learn.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.structure: There is no point in + verb-ing
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: It's no use + verb-ing; What's the point of...?
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using to verb, forgetting in.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "There's no point in waiting.", "There's no point arguing.", "What's the point of trying?"
examples: There's no point in crying over spilt milk.
There's no point calling him now; he's asleep.
I think there's no point in worrying.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: The more I practice, the better I get; The less... the less.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Missing "the", wrong word order.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "The more you work, the more you earn.", "The sooner, the better.", "The older I get, the wiser I become."
examples: The more you practice, the easier it becomes.
The faster you drive, the more fuel you use.
The louder she spoke, the less they listened.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: It's worth it; Is it worth...?; worth + noun (It's worth a try).
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using to verb, worth to visit -> worth visiting.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "It's worth visiting.", "It's not worth waiting.", "Is it worth the money?"
examples: The film is worth watching.
It's not worth getting angry.
It's worth a try.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: I look forward to (formal); really looking forward.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using to + base verb (I'm looking forward to see you), missing to.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I'm looking forward to the party.", "Looking forward to hearing from you.", "She's looking forward to meeting you."
examples: I'm looking forward to the weekend.
We're looking forward to working with you.
Are you looking forward to your trip?

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.structure: I'm used to / I can't get used to + noun/verb-ing
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.pattern: I'm used to + noun/verb-ing; I can't get used to + noun/verb-ing
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: Not used to; get used to.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using base verb after to.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I'm used to the heat.", "I can't get used to driving on the left.", "You'll get used to it."
examples: I'm not used to getting up early.
She can't get used to the silence.
He's still not used to his new job.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: Due to + noun; Because of + noun.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using because and so together.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I left because I was tired.", "Since you're here, let's start.", "As it was raining, we stayed in."
examples: She's late because the traffic was bad.
Because I was hungry, I ate a sandwich.
As it's your birthday, I'll pay.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.when_to_use: When describing extreme situation and consequence.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: Such + noun phrase + that.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using too ... that, mixing such and so.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "It was so hot that we couldn't sleep.", "She ran so fast that I couldn't catch her."
examples: I was so tired that I fell asleep on the bus.
The music was so loud that my ears hurt.
He spoke so quickly that no one understood.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Explaining why something can't be done.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: far too, much too.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using too much + adj, using "to" incorrectly.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "It's too heavy to lift.", "You're too young to drive.", "The coffee is too hot to drink."
examples: She's too sick to go out.
This box is too big to fit in the car.
He's too proud to apologize.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Saying quantity or quality is below needed level.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: enough ... for; enough ... to + verb.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Word order (enough big -> big enough).
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I don't have enough time.", "She's not old enough.", "Is it warm enough to swim?"
examples: We haven't got enough money.
He isn't tall enough to reach.
There aren't enough chairs.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.structure: I have no idea + wh-clause / about
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: I've no idea; I have no clue.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using that, word order.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I have no idea what you're talking about.", "I have no idea where he went.", "I have no idea how to do that."
examples: I have no idea what time it is.
She has no idea who called.
I have no idea why he's angry.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: I was wondering if... (more polite request).
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using question word order after if.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I wonder if he'll come.", "I was wondering if you could help me.", "I wonder where she is."
examples: I wonder if it will rain.
I wonder what they're doing.
I was wondering if I could borrow your car.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: Would it be possible to...; Is there any chance...?
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using can with Is it possible? unnecessarily.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "Is it possible to get a refund?", "Would it be possible to change the date?", "Is it possible to park here?"
examples: Is it possible to speak to the manager?
Is it possible to check out late?
Would it be possible to have a window seat?

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.structure: I'm sure / I'm not sure + clause
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.pattern: I'm sure (that) + clause; I'm not sure if/whether/wh...
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: I'm positive; I doubt if.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using surely incorrectly.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I'm sure you'll love it.", "I'm not sure if he's coming.", "Are you sure about that?"
examples: I'm sure it's going to be fun.
I'm not sure where I left my keys.
He's not sure whether to go.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.when_to_use: Before giving a negative or honest view.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: To tell you the truth; Honestly; Frankly.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using it with positive obvious truths unnecessarily.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "To be honest, I didn't like it.", "To be honest, I'm not really interested.", "To be honest, I was a bit disappointed."
examples: To be honest, I think it's too expensive.
To be honest, I forgot about the meeting.
To be honest, I'm exhausted.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: From my perspective; As I see it; Personally, I think.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using according to me (incorrect).
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "In my opinion, it's a waste of time.", "In my opinion, we should wait."
examples: In my opinion, this is the best solution.
In my opinion, the movie was too long.
In my opinion, everyone should learn a language.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.when_to_use: News, reports, not for personal opinion.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: Based on; As per (formal).
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: According to me (wrong); using for opinion.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "According to the weather forecast, it will rain.", "According to John, the meeting is cancelled."
examples: According to the newspaper, prices are rising.
According to the recipe, we need three eggs.
According to him, everything is fine.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: To the best of my knowledge; As far as I can tell.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using for definite facts.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "As far as I know, she still works there.", "As far as I know, it's free."
examples: As far as I know, the flight is on time.
As far as I know, he's single.
As far as I'm aware, no decision has been made.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: The reason is that... (more formal).
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: The reason is because... (redundant but accepted in spoken), using The reason why... is because... same.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "The reason why I'm late is because I missed the bus.", "The reason I called is because I need your help."
examples: The reason I'm tired is because I stayed up late.
The reason she quit is because of the stress.
The reason why we chose this hotel is because it's cheap.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.when_to_use: To stress that both are true.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: ...but...also; Subject + not only + verb + but also...
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Forgetting inversion when "Not only" starts a sentence.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "Not only is she smart, but she's also kind.", "He not only plays the guitar but also sings."
examples: Not only did we arrive late, but we also forgot the tickets.
She's not only a teacher but also a writer.
The trip was not only fun but also educational.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.structure: The first time I + past simple
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.pattern: The first time I + [past verb phrase], + [past result]
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: The first time I saw/met/went.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using present perfect.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "The first time I saw her, I knew...", "The first time I went abroad was in 2010."
examples: The first time I ate sushi, I didn't like it.
The first time I drove a car, I was terrified.
The first time we met, we talked all night.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.core_meaning: Long time since something last happened.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.when_to_use: When you haven't done/something for a long time.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.pattern: It's + been + ages / a long time + since + [past simple]
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: It's years since I saw him; It's been a while.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using present perfect after since.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "It's been ages since we last spoke.", "It's years since I went to the cinema."
examples: It's been ages since I had a proper holiday.
It's a long time since I felt this happy.
It's been ages since I saw you!

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.when_to_use: To admit something you can't control.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: She couldn't help laughing; I can't help it.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using to verb, or I can't help but + base (both possible but verb-ing is simpler).
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I can't help feeling anxious.", "I can't help laughing.", "I couldn't help overhearing."
examples: I can't help thinking about him.
She can't help eating chocolate.
I couldn't help notice your accent.

chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.core_meaning: Emphasizing impossibility of a greater thing.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.primary_communicative_function: Highlighting contrast between simple and difficult.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.when_to_use: To say "A is impossible, so B is definitely impossible".
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.key_variations: I can't even walk, let alone run.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.common_learner_mistakes: Using positive form, word order.
chunk_enabling_grammar_patterns.chunk_compatibility: "I can't afford a bike, let alone a car.", "He can't boil an egg, let alone cook a dinner."
examples: She can't read, let alone write.
